payload.formula_text:
Churn Probability = Klaviyo Predictive Analytics Score (0-100%)
Risk Segment = Bucketed probability ranges
payload.formula_explanation: Uses Klaviyo predictive analytics to segment customers by their probability of churning. The donut chart shows the distribution across risk tiers, helping prioritize retention efforts on high-risk valuable customers.
how_to_read: Green slice shows low-risk customers (<25% churn probability) - your loyal base
Orange slice shows medium-risk customers (25-50%) - worth monitoring
Red/dark slices show high-risk customers (>50%) - require immediate attention
Center number shows total customers with churn predictions
Click any slice to filter and view customers in that risk segment
decisions_answered: What percentage of our customers are at high churn risk?
How many customers need immediate retention intervention?
Is our churn risk profile healthy or concerning?
Which risk segment should we prioritize for campaigns?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows your customer base segmented by predicted churn risk.

Green is your safe zone - low risk customers who are likely to keep buying. Orange is your watch list - they could go either way. Red and dark red are your danger zone - high probability of never purchasing again.

The key insight is not just the proportions, but the absolute numbers. If you have 10,000 customers in high risk segments, that's revenue walking out the door.

Use this to prioritize. High-value customers in the red zone deserve personal outreach. Medium risk customers might respond to a discount. Low risk customers can receive standard nurturing.

payload.formula_text:
Retention Rate = (Customers with >1 Order / Total Cohort Customers) * 100
Cohort = All customers whose first purchase was in a given month
payload.formula_explanation: Groups customers by the month of their first purchase (cohort) and measures what percentage made a repeat purchase. Higher retention rates indicate more effective customer acquisition and product-market fit.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a monthly cohort of first-time customers
The percentage shows what portion of that cohort made repeat purchases
Higher bars indicate better retention for that acquisition month
Compare recent cohorts to historical ones to spot trend changes
Labels display exact retention percentages for precision
decisions_answered: Which acquisition months produced the most loyal customers?
Is our customer quality improving or declining over time?
Did specific marketing campaigns drive better retention?
What is our baseline retention rate across cohorts?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows retention rates by customer acquisition cohort.

Each bar represents customers who made their first purchase in a specific month. The height shows what percentage came back for a second purchase.

Strong cohorts have high retention - 40% or more is excellent for most e-commerce. Weak cohorts under 25% signal problems with that month's acquisition quality or product experience.

Compare cohorts over time. If recent cohorts have lower retention, investigate. Was there a marketing change? Product issue? This helps you understand not just if customers are staying, but when they were acquired matters.

payload.formula_text:
First Purchase Revenue = SUM(Order Value) WHERE Order Number = 1
Repeat Purchase Revenue = SUM(Order Value) WHERE Order Number > 1
payload.formula_explanation: Compares revenue generated from first-time purchases versus repeat purchases over the last 30 days. A healthy business typically sees 40-60% of revenue from repeat customers, indicating strong retention.
how_to_read: Blue bar shows revenue from customers making their first purchase
Green bar shows revenue from returning customers
Longer repeat bar indicates strong retention and customer loyalty
Below each bar, see order counts and average order values
Compare AOV between segments - repeat customers often spend more
decisions_answered: What percentage of revenue comes from repeat customers?
Is our revenue mix healthy (40-60% from repeat)?
Do repeat customers have higher average order values?
Should we invest more in acquisition or retention?
payload.narration_text:
This comparison chart shows where your revenue comes from - first purchases versus repeat purchases.

The blue bar is new customer revenue. The green bar is revenue from customers who've bought before. For a healthy e-commerce business, repeat revenue should be 40-60% of total.

If first purchase dominates, you might have a retention problem - customers aren't coming back. If repeat dominates heavily, you might have an acquisition problem - not enough new customers entering the funnel.

The AOV comparison below matters too. If repeat customers spend more per order, that's proof that retention investments pay off. Your best customers get better over time.

payload.formula_text:
Purchasing Customers = COUNT(DISTINCT PERSON_ID) WHERE TYPE = "Placed Order"
Growth Rate = ((Current Month - Previous Month) / Previous Month) * 100
payload.formula_explanation: This chart tracks the number of unique customers who made purchases each month over the last 12 months. It provides a direct view of customer engagement and helps identify trends in repeat purchase behavior.
how_to_read: The blue line shows the count of unique customers making purchases each month
Upward trends indicate improving customer retention and engagement
Downward trends may signal issues with customer satisfaction or marketing effectiveness
Hover over any point to see the exact customer count for that month
Click a point to filter the page to that specific month
decisions_answered: Is our customer base growing or shrinking over time?
Are there seasonal patterns in customer purchasing behavior?
When did significant changes in customer engagement occur?
Should we launch re-engagement campaigns based on declining trends?
payload.narration_text:
This chart tracks your purchasing customer count over 12 months.

The blue line shows unique customers making purchases each month. An upward trend means you're keeping customers engaged and coming back. A downward trend signals potential problems.

Look for patterns. Q4 often spikes for retail due to holidays. Post-holiday dips in January are normal. But sustained decline needs attention.

This answers: Is our customer base healthy? When did changes happen? And should we launch win-back campaigns?

payload.formula_text:
Days Between Orders = Current Order Date - Previous Order Date
Distribution = COUNT(Customers) per time bucket
payload.formula_explanation: Calculates the gap between consecutive purchases for repeat customers and groups them into time buckets. This reveals purchase velocity patterns and helps identify optimal re-engagement timing windows.
how_to_read: Each bar shows how many customers fall into that time gap bucket
Shorter time gaps (0-30 days) indicate highly engaged repeat customers
Longer gaps (180+ days) may represent at-risk or infrequent buyers
The distribution shape reveals your typical purchase cycle
Click any bar to explore customers in that time bucket
decisions_answered: What is the typical time between customer purchases?
When is the optimal time to send re-engagement emails?
How many customers have long purchase gaps requiring intervention?
Is our purchase velocity improving with retention efforts?
payload.narration_text:
This distribution chart reveals how long customers wait between purchases.

The 0-30 day bucket is your power users - highly engaged customers who buy frequently. The 31-60 and 61-90 day buckets are your regular customers. Beyond 90 days, customers are becoming infrequent, and past 180 days, they're at risk of churning.

Use this to time your marketing. If most customers naturally repurchase at 45 days, send your reminder at 40 days. If you see a large 180+ day bucket, those customers need win-back campaigns now.

This tells you when to intervene and how to structure your email flows.
